preFix.title: Spinning merino for lace
preFix.subtitle: Drafting a 2-ply lace-weight yarn at 600 wraps per 10 cm from fine merino top
preFix.excerpt: Lace-weight yarn demands a very fine, consistently Z-twisted single spun from 18 to 21 micron merino or BFL top, then plied S-twist and blocked to bloom the halo and set the twist.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from spinning references including Abby Franquemont 'Respect the Spindle', Judith MacKenzie on fine-fibre spinning, and general WPI-based yarn-weight classification practice.

content.text: Lace-weight yarn is characterised by its fineness: a 2-ply lace-weight typically measures 30 to 40 WPI (wraps per inch) when plied, which translates to roughly 600 wraps per 10 cm on the single before plying. The single must be Z-twisted at a high angle, then plied S-twist at a slightly lower twist level to produce a balanced, drapey yarn. Fine
merino top

at 18 to 21 microns is the standard fibre for this project. BFL (Bluefaced Leicester) top is a reliable alternative if you prefer a crisper yarn with less halo. This is advanced work because maintaining consistency over the length of a lace single demands sustained attention to drafting rhythm.

attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing back from the drive band, flyer, and bobbin. Tie hair back and roll sleeves before sitting at the wheel.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

items.name: Fine merino top, 18–21 micron (or BFL top)
Saxony or castle wheel with a high-ratio whorl
Soft measuring tape or WPI gauge

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-draft the top

Strip the top braid lengthwise into ribbons roughly 1 cm wide. Then pre-draft each ribbon by holding it at both ends and pulling the hands apart until the ribbon narrows to about 3 mm across, even and translucent when held to the light. Pre-drafting removes the step of attenuating thick commercial top at the wheel and lets you focus on twist control while spinning.
Step 2: Spin the singles with a long draw
Set a high-ratio whorl (14:1 or faster) and a light take-up tension. Use a
long draw
: hold the fibre supply in one hand and pull the drafting hand back 30 to 40 cm in a single smooth motion while the wheel inserts
Z-twist
throughout the draft zone. Release the fibre supply hand to allow the last inches to attenuate, then wind onto the bobbin. The
WPI
of the single before plying should read 50 to 60 on a gauge or ruler. Check every 10 to 15 minutes by winding a short section around the gauge. Adjust the draft length to correct drift.
Step 3: Fill two bobbins evenly

Spin two bobbins of singles to be plied together. When possible, fill them to approximately equal weight so the ply is balanced end-to-end. If one bobbin runs out during plying, break off, re-join to the remaining single, and continue with a fresh strand.
Step 4: Ply with S-twist
Reverse the wheel direction to insert
S-twist

. Hold both singles together with light tension, keeping them parallel rather than crossed. Ply at a slightly lower twist ratio than you used for the singles: if the singles whorl was 14:1, a 10:1 or 12:1 whorl for plying will give a balanced 2-ply. Test for balance by letting a short loop of the plied yarn hang free from the needle: a balanced yarn hangs with no coil, a twist-heavy yarn coils back on itself, and an under-plied yarn simply droops open.
Step 5: Finish and block the skein

Wind the 2-ply yarn onto the niddy-noddy, counting circuits to calculate yardage. Tie in four places. Soak in 30 °C water for 20 minutes. Lift out, squeeze gently, and hang with a small weight to pull the skein straight as it dries.
Blocking
the skein in warm water with a snap sets the twist and opens up the halo of the merino, giving the yarn the characteristic softness and lustre of a commercially produced lace weight.

items.fix: Slow the backward draw. Allow the twist to travel through the attenuating zone before extending further. Increase whorl ratio if needed.
items.cause: Draw length exceeds the staple length before enough twist has entered the drafting zone
items.problem: Singles keep breaking during long draw

items.fix: Pre-draft more aggressively and check WPI of the single before committing a full bobbin. The single WPI should read 50 or above.
items.cause: Singles were not fine enough before plying
items.problem: Plied yarn is too thick (below 30 WPI)

items.fix: Reduce treadle count per arm-length of ply, or switch to a slower whorl for plying.
items.cause: Over-plied: too much S-twist was added relative to the Z-twist in the singles
items.problem: Yarn coils when held in a loop after plying

items.fix: Check WPI more frequently while spinning. Set a consistent treadle rhythm before starting each long draw.
items.cause: Draft length and treadle speed were not matched consistently
